title: Recursion concept

publication.changeSummary: Topic-specific draft for Recursion concept; requires human source, technical, pedagogy, and accessibility review. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: CS2023 Software Development Fundamentals; August 2023; section applicable to Recursion concept
sources.claim: A recursive definition solves a problem using a base case and calls on strictly smaller or otherwise progressing instances, with each call receiving its own frame.

canonicalExplanation.summary: A recursive definition solves a problem using a base case and calls on strictly smaller or otherwise progressing instances, with each call receiving its own frame.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: Factorial of three calls factorial of two, then one, reaches the base result, and multiplies returned values while frames unwind. Your goal is to trace a simple recursive definition and identify a missing or unreachable base case. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Iteration; Decomposition; Calls, parameters and returns; Selection. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Operational model
sections.body: A recursive definition solves a problem using a base case and calls on strictly smaller or otherwise progressing instances, with each call receiving its own frame. The learner should use this model to predict an observable result rather than merely repeat terminology.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure point
sections.body: A reachable base case is necessary but not sufficient: every recursive path must progress toward it, and deep recursion can exceed runtime stack limits. A correct explanation therefore includes the applicable assumptions and avoids this common mistake: Adding any base-case branch guarantees termination even if recursive calls keep the same argument.

includes: Understand base cases and smaller self-similar calls without requiring language syntax.
Trace a simple recursive definition and identify a missing or unreachable base case.
excludes: A reachable base case is necessary but not sufficient: every recursive path must progress toward it, and deep recursion can exceed runtime stack limits.

outcomes: Trace a simple recursive definition and identify a missing or unreachable base case.

examples.title: Recursion concept: direct case
examples.situation: Factorial of three calls factorial of two, then one, reaches the base result, and multiplies returned values while frames unwind.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the starting evidence: Factorial of three calls factorial of two, then one, reaches the base result, and multiplies returned values while frames unwind.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Check the result against this correction: Check both reachability and progress: each recursive path must move a well-founded measure toward a base case before the call can return.
examples.result: The case supports the declared outcome because Check both reachability and progress: each recursive path must move a well-founded measure toward a base case before the call can return.

examples.title: Recursion concept: boundary case
examples.situation: A folder-size routine returns a file's size directly and recursively sums each child only when the current item is a folder.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed context: A folder-size routine returns a file's size directly and recursively sums each child only when the current item is a folder.
Step 2 — Keep this boundary explicit: A reachable base case is necessary but not sufficient: every recursive path must progress toward it, and deep recursion can exceed runtime stack limits.
Step 3 — Reject the documented misconception: Adding any base-case branch guarantees termination even if recursive calls keep the same argument.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Trace a simple recursive definition and identify a missing or unreachable base case.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Adding any base-case branch guarantees termination even if recursive calls keep the same argument.
misconceptions.correction: Check both reachability and progress: each recursive path must move a well-founded measure toward a base case before the call can return.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using the direct and boundary scenarios, explain why this belief fails: Adding any base-case branch guarantees termination even if recursive calls keep the same argument.

analogy.example: A service counter can model Recursion concept: it advertises accepted requests and results while its internal work remains behind a named interface.
usefulFor: Separating contracts, local work, calls, returned results, and repeated service use
limitations: A service counter does not capture language-specific frames, reference semantics, closures, or recursion limits.

recap.summary: You should now be able to trace a simple recursive definition and identify a missing or unreachable base case. Keep this boundary in view: A reachable base case is necessary but not sufficient: every recursive path must progress toward it, and deep recursion can exceed runtime stack limits.
retrievalPrompts: Explain Recursion concept without using the optional analogy and include one observable consequence.
Why is this statement incorrect: Adding any base-case branch guarantees termination even if recursive calls keep the same argument.
Apply the model to this case from memory: A folder-size routine returns a file's size directly and recursively sums each child only when the current item is a folder.
